# Ezekiel 23: When God's Judgment Becomes Inevitable

God confronts Ezekiel with a parable so graphic, so unflinching in its vulgarity, that many readers recoil. Two sisters—Samaria and Jerusalem—are depicted as prostitutes who abandoned their covenant relationship with God to chase after foreign powers. The relentless imagery is deliberate. This is not sanitized moral instruction. God is making Ezekiel, and through him the people, witness the full degradation of what Israel had become. They were chosen, called Mine by God, yet they systematically chose humiliation with empire after empire—Assyria, Babylon, Egypt—trading the exclusive covenant of the God who created them for the hollow embrace of nations that used and discarded them. What makes this chapter so brutal is not the sexual language itself but what it represents: the soul-destroying choice to abandon ultimate allegiance to God for the temporary intoxication of worldly power and validation.

The tragedy deepens when we see that Jerusalem, the younger sister Oholibah, had the cautionary example of Samaria's destruction right before her eyes. Samaria fell to Assyria; the text tells us she "became a byword among women" and "they executed judgment against her." Yet Jerusalem saw this and did worse. She increased her infidelity. She did not learn. She lusted after the very powers who had already proven themselves her destroyers. She even sent for Babylonian men, desiring them before they came—manufacturing her own ruin through deliberate choice. What arrests us here is the refusal to repent, the multiplication of unfaithfulness even in the face of catastrophe. This is not ignorance; it is willful self-deception, the hardening of a heart that has chosen a lie so thoroughly that it will not turn back even when the consequences stand plain before it.

God's judgment arrives with a specificity that cannot be misunderstood. He says He will hand Jerusalem over to the very lovers she lusted for, and they will come "from every side" to execute His judgment. The cruelty she will face—mutilation, slaughter, the loss of children, exposure of shame—is presented as the direct reversal of her seduction. The hands that caressed her will cut her. The nakedness she exposed will remain naked. But the deepest cut comes at the end: God says this judgment comes because she "cast Me behind your back" and "forgotten Me." This is the core transgression. Not merely that she sinned, but that she made God secondary, substituted His presence with foreign gods, and profaned even His sanctuary and Sabbaths by performing abominable rituals within His own house. The physical judgment is real, but it flows from a spiritual abandonment that no political maneuvering can undo.

What faces us in this chapter is a terrifying principle: God takes seriously the allegiance He asks from His people. When we know Him, when we have been claimed by Him, there is no neutral ground. The pursuit of other powers, the worship of other gods, the elevation of worldly validation above loyalty to our Covenant God—these are not small infractions they are adultery. And God, in His jealous love for the people He has claimed, will not leave such unfaithfulness unaddressed. The chapter does not end with an invitation to change; it ends with the pronouncement of consequence. The moment for Jerusalem's repentance, it seems, has passed. She will drink the cup of her sister's judgment. We are called to ask ourselves: what have we cast behind our backs? What temporary power, validation, or comfort have we chosen over undivided allegiance to God? And are we watching this history so that we might turn back while we still can?
